reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

/no_think 下面是一个 FNM unresolved cluster。请只返回 JSON 数组，不要解释。 本次只允许动作：match、ignore_ref、needs_review。 若截图已经能明确判断坏掉的数字或同页注释，请直接输出可自动落地的动作，不要退回 needs_review。 { "cluster_id": "toc-manual-2-11-11-errors-and-projection:toc-manual-2-11-11-errors-and-projection:footnote", "chapter_title": "11. Errors and projections", "page_range": [ 442, 486 ], "note_system": "footnote", "request_mode": "anchor_rebind", "allowed_actions": [ "match", "ignore_ref", "needs_review" ], "page_contexts": [ { "page_no": 440, "ocr_excerpt": "made significant ones more difficult to perceive. Since we still lack an accurate description of how Qing “madness laws” appeared and changed and what kind of object they targeted, our comparison of law with medicine and other Qing fields suffers accordingly. Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. Gravity was itself assessed by measuring the degree of intention of the culprit, the kinship proximity between offender and victim, and any other circumstance that officials found relevant. To mete out sentences, legal officials deployed categories such as the “six killings” (11.1) and assessed criminal intention or the relative status of offender and victim. Legal practice had to respect certain procedures (e.g., the necessity to obtain a criminal’s confession before sentencing) and took place within institutional constraints—the annual review of death sentences at the Autumn Assizes, multiple sources of law, the proliferation of ad hoc substitutes, etc. To consid ..." }, { "page_no": 442, "ocr_excerpt": "between intentional (gu 故) and non-intentional (sheng 售) acts. $ ^{4} $ This distinction was later preserved in successive codes under different names. $ ^{5} $ Before I explain how Qing homicide laws were structured according to their degree of intention, yet did not rely on the principle of mens rea, let me briefly explain the terms by which Qing law discussed intentions. A network of key categories defined the semantic field of criminal intention in Qing homicide laws. Xin 心, a word I have translated as “Heart” in medical texts, is best rendered as “mind” or “intent” in legal contexts because legal discussion of xin surrounded intentions, not the functioning of any organ. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). The six killings: a hierarchy of intentions Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer. “Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “ ..." }, { "page_no": 444, "ocr_excerpt": "person was what differentiated “killing by design” from “intentional killing” (故殺), which applied when a killer’s homicidal intent was “not known to others” (非人所知). $ ^{10} $ No matter whether this intent to kill was premeditated or arose during a fight (臨時有意欲殺), the lone culprit was judged as having committed “intentional killing” and was sentenced to the same punishment as for “Killing by design”: effective decapitation after the Assizes. “Killing in an affray” (鬪殺) meant killing unintentionally during a fight. $ ^{11} $ In Qing law, intent to kill had become the main difference between “killing in an affray” and “intentional killing.” As a result, those who had killed during a fight had to demonstrate that they had not intended to kill their victim. $ ^{12} $ Under most circumstances, the ensuing sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (絞監候) was eventually commuted to the highest sentence below death, namely exile to 3000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy bamboo. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $ Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). ### 11.2 The first Qing laws on madness: creating a new legal object An early case of parricide The earliest Qing case of ..." }, { "page_no": 447, "ocr_excerpt": "because it was sentenced in a way that differed from (as far as I know) all earlier and later cases of parricide in a state of madness in Chinese imperial history, and because it involved a particular class of people that was unique to the early Qing. $ ^{22} $ Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). unmatched_anchors.source_text: A network of key categories defined the semantic field of criminal intention in Qing homicide laws. Xin 心, a word I have translated as “Heart” in medical texts, is best rendered as “mind” or “intent” in legal contexts because legal discussion of xin surrounded intentions, not the functioning of any organ. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). Gu 故 was used adverbially in “killing intentionally” (故殺).

Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer.
(謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected
main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

“Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in the actual killing. $ ^{9} $ The participation of more than one

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. Gravity was itself assessed by measuring the degree of intention of the culprit, the kinship proximity between offender and victim, and any other circumstance that officials found relevant. To mete out sentences, legal officials deployed categories such as the “six killings” (11.1) and assessed criminal intention or the relative status of offender and victim. Legal practice had to respect certain procedures (e.g., the necessity to obtain a criminal’s confession before sentencing) and took place within institutional constraints—the annual review of death sentences at the Autumn Assizes, multiple sources of law, the proliferation of ad hoc substitutes, etc. To consider the role of all these factors in the formation of the legal object “madness illness” is to appreciate the inseparability of “the meaning and materiality of law.”

between intentional (gu 故) and non-intentional (sheng 售) acts. $ ^{4} $ This distinction was later preserved in successive codes under different names. $ ^{5} $ Before I explain how Qing homicide laws were structured according to their degree of intention, yet did not rely on the principle of mens rea, let me briefly explain the terms by which Qing law discussed intentions.

The 1667 and 1669 laws concerned only homicides, not madness and mad people in general. The 1669 rule in particular integrated “killing because of madness” within the six killings. Note that in both rate and rationale, the compensation the 1669 rule sought for “killing because of madness” differed significantly from the privilege of monetary redemption granted to some disabled people in statute 22 of the Qing code, which was called “Allowing redemption for the old, the young, and the disabled” (老小廢疾收贖). $ ^{31} $ Under statute 22, one could redeem a death sentence with 0.525 ounces of silver, much less that the 12.42 ounces sought for “killing because of madness.” $ ^{32} $ People who were the object of legalized pity under statute 22 could redeem a real sentence with money; whereas in the case of “[people with] madness illness killing another,” the payment was the sentence, imposed for an inadvertent act that had led to a grave outcome. Compassion was not a factor in the 1669 rule, which likened “killing because of madness” to “accidental killing” on the basis of their common lack of criminal intention. $ ^{33} $

Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $

of madness” was integrated into the network of the six killings. $ ^{30} $ From then on, and until the end of the Qing, killings committed in a fit of insanity were legally viewed as lacking both awareness and criminal intention.

In imperial Chinese law, a “penal denomination” was not the charge brought against one at the onset of a trial, but the name of the crime for which one was sentenced. Officials selected the fittest “crime name” by assessing the circumstances of the crime, but “[o]nce an offense was judged to fall into a certain category of crime [罪名], the punishment was predetermined.” $ ^{27} $ Both ideally and in practice, the officials who administered this system of fixed punishments were concerned “to maintain a balance between the offense and the penalty.” This judicial approach differed from that of physicians, who gave little importance to denominations in identifying the cause of an illness, and who aimed to put an end to pathological acts rather than find an apt punishment for them.

We should not confuse non-declaration with secrecy. $ ^{119} $ Madness was probably a problem in the treatment of which strangers were not welcome to meddle, but it could hardly be kept secret. People who suddenly turned mad attracted the most attention, as when the friends and relatives of a newly crazy man “filled the hall” (親朋滿座) and asked a visiting physician what could be done. $ ^{120} $ Since wacky speech, wild cursing, and violent fits could hardly go unnoticed in small communities, neighbors and local leaders usually knew when someone suffered from madness. Bai Shengqi was known as “madman Bai” 白瘋子 in surrounding villages even if he had been confined at home for five or six years. $ ^{121} $

Since we still lack an accurate description of how Qing “madness laws” appeared and changed and what kind of object they targeted, our comparison of law with medicine and other Qing fields suffers accordingly. Few have paid attention to the ways in which the legal discourse on madness was generated by specific judicial notions, procedures, and concerns. $ ^{3} $ One of the most important tasks of the legal system was to punish criminal acts in proportion of their gravity. Gravity was itself assessed by measuring the degree of intention of the culprit, the kinship proximity between offender and victim, and any other circumstance that officials found relevant. To mete out sentences, legal officials deployed categories such as the “six killings” (11.1) and assessed criminal intention or the relative status of offender and victim. Legal practice had to respect certain procedures (e.g., the necessity to obtain a criminal’s confession before sentencing) and took place within institutional constraints—the annual review of death sentences at the Autumn Assizes, multiple sources of law, the proliferation of ad hoc substitutes, etc. To consider the role of all these factors in the formation of the legal object “madness illness” is to appreciate the inseparability of “the meaning and materiality of law.” This chapter explains how a new cri ...

between intentional (gu 故) and non-intentional (sheng 售) acts. $ ^{4} $ This distinction was later preserved in successive codes under different names. $ ^{5} $ Before I explain how Qing homicide laws were structured according to their degree of intention, yet did not rely on the principle of mens rea, let me briefly explain the terms by which Qing law discussed intentions. A network of key categories defined the semantic field of criminal intention in Qing homicide laws. Xin 心, a word I have translated as “Heart” in medical texts, is best rendered as “mind” or “intent” in legal contexts because legal discussion of xin surrounded intentions, not the functioning of any organ. The term xin appeared in expressions like “intentionally” (or “having the intention to”: 有心), “intent upon...” (… 之心) or a combination of both. $ ^{6} $ Yi 意 could be used similarly (as in the phrase “there was initially no intention to harm another” 初無害人之意), but it was also used to describe “unexpected” (出於不意) actions. Yi appeared in the technical phrase “the one who devised the idea [of killing]” (造意者) or in combination with yu 欲 in “there was an intention to kill” (有意欲殺). Gu 故 was used adverbially in “killing intentionally” (故殺). These terms formed the core of numerous legal rules and they were deployed in myriads of judicial cases through history, from the county level where the first provisional senten ...

intention was deployed particularly clearly in the network of laws on homicides in which “killing because of madness” would be integrated. The six killings: a hierarchy of intentions Unlike modern Western legal systems, which distinguish between murder and manslaughter or between degrees of homicide, Chinese law in imperial times called all homicides “killings” (sha 殺). Legal commentators customarily divided killings into sub-types. In descending order of gravity, the “Six killings” (六殺) were mou sha 謀殺 (“Killing by design” or “Plotting to kill”), gu sha 故殺 (“Killing intentionally”), dou sha 鬭殺 (“Killing in an affray”), wu sha 誤殺 (“Killing by mistake”), xi sha 戲殺 (“Killing in play”), and guoshi sha 過失殺 (“Killing accidentally”). Three statutes (lǔ 律) sufficed to define killings in the Ming and Qing codes. $ ^{7} $ Here I explain how the “Six killings” applied to the killing of a single person unrelated to the killer. “Killing by design” (謀殺), which was understood as the result of prior scheming (謀) between two or more people, was considered the gravest of the six. $ ^{8} $ The law expected such plots to occur in vendettas, fights over property, or schemes to kill one's own spouse. “The one who devised the idea” (造意者) was to be sentenced to decapitation after the Autumn Assizes (斬監後), where it would be classified as “effective” (情實) even if the main plotter had not taken part in ...

person was what differentiated “killing by design” from “intentional killing” (故殺), which applied when a killer’s homicidal intent was “not known to others” (非人所知). $ ^{10} $ No matter whether this intent to kill was premeditated or arose during a fight (臨時有意欲殺), the lone culprit was judged as having committed “intentional killing” and was sentenced to the same punishment as for “Killing by design”: effective decapitation after the Assizes. “Killing in an affray” (鬪殺) meant killing unintentionally during a fight. $ ^{11} $ In Qing law, intent to kill had become the main difference between “killing in an affray” and “intentional killing.” As a result, those who had killed during a fight had to demonstrate that they had not intended to kill their victim. $ ^{12} $ Under most circumstances, the ensuing sentence of “strangulation after the Assizes” (絞監候) was eventually commuted to the highest sentence below death, namely exile to 3000 li preceded by 100 strokes of the heavy bamboo. In the three remaining killings, which were all discussed in a single statute, death was understood as being inflicted involuntarily. $ ^{13} $ “Killing in play” (戲殺) occurred in the course of dangerous activities in which people engaged purposely, like sword-practice. $ ^{14} $ This rare crime was punished by “strangulation after the Assizes,” but classified as “suspended” (緩決) at the Assizes, resulting ...

mischance had occurred, or, as commentator Shen Zhiqi 沈之奇 specified in the early eighteenth century, on the intention that motivated the act that led to the mistaken killing. $ ^{15} $ “Accidental killing” (過失殺) could be redeemed by conferring 12.42 ounces of silver to the family of the victim to provide for burial fees. $ ^{16} $ This punishment was mild because the law recognized that the culprit had lacked any will to kill, harm, or even partake in dangerous activities. Compensation for a life taken was paid with money, not with one’s own life. Since “Killing because of madness” in the early Qing was sentenced in the same way as “accidental killing,” let me cite the entire text of the relevant statute. $ ^{17} $ Anyone who kills or injures another accidentally $ ^{18} $ (this is comparatively less serious than killing in play) will be sentenced as if for killing or injuring in an affray. [The privilege of] monetary redemption will be granted according to law, and the money will be given to the family (of the person who was killed or injured). (Accident means that which the ear or eye does not extend to, or what was not contemplated. For example, when one is shooting wild animals or when, for a reason, one is tossing bricks or tiles and unexpectedly kills another; or again if, when one is climbing on precipitous [terrain], one's foot slips and one falls, thus involving [i.e., ...

fright, or is driving a cart at a rapid speed on a descent, when the situation makes it so that one cannot stop; $ ^{19} $ or if several persons are carrying a heavy object and the strength [of one] is insufficient to control it so that the other persons carrying it are hurt. In all [these] cases where initially there was no intention to $ barm^{20} $ another, but which led by chance to the killing or injuring of another, sentence as if for killing or wounding in an affray, and grant [the privilege of] monetary redemption according to law. [The silver] will be given to the household [of the person] who was killed or injured, to serve as funds for burial or medical treatment.) 若過失殺傷人者 （較戲殺愈輕） 各准鬥殺傷罪 依律收贖 給付其 （被殺傷之） 家 （過失 謂耳目所不及 思慮所不到 如彈射禽獸 因事投擲甎瓦 不期而殺人者 或因升高險足有蹉跌 累及同伴 或駕船使風乘馬驚走 馳車下坡 勢不能止 或共舉重物 力不能制 損及同舉物者 凡初無害人之意 而偶致殺傷人者 皆准鬬毆殺傷人罪 依律收贖 給付被殺傷之家 以為營葬及醫藥之資）
The clause specifying that death could not be foreseen pointed to the difference between accidental killing and “Killing in play.” Accidental death was entirely coincidental.
### 11.2 The first Qing laws on madness: creating a new legal object An early case of parricide The earliest Qing case of madness I have identified was a somewhat enigmatic parricide that took place in 1654, about ten years after the Manchus had seized Beijing and officially named it the capital of the newly established ...

because it was sentenced in a way that differed from (as far as I know) all earlier and later cases of parricide in a state of madness in Chinese imperial history, and because it involved a particular class of people that was unique to the early Qing. $ ^{22} $ Xu San 徐三, son of Xu Jiuwei 徐九緯, was a “hunter” (打牲人) under the supervision of Palace eunuch Shen Maode 沈茂德. “Hunters” (Manchu: butha; Ch.: buteba 布特哈) was the generic name given to several non-Manchu tribes in an area that is now at the confines of Inner Mongolia and Heilongjiang in northern China. $ ^{23} $ One day with a knife Xu San stabbed (戳) his father, who died shortly thereafter, but before he died, the father had time to testify that “his son had been suffering from madness illness” (伊子原有瘋疾). The Ministry of Punishments approved the sentence that an original memorial (now lost) had proposed: Xu San was exempted of any crime (免罪) because he was judged to have killed “because of madness illness” (因瘋疾). This decision had no other legal repercussions because it was not established as a precedent for judging future cases of a similar nature. $ ^{24} $ Since this affair concerned tribes under the direct supervision of Manchu institutions like the Imperial Clan and Imperial Household Departments, the memorial reporting the crime may have been written in Manchu before it was translated into Chinese. As I suggested abov ...

Shunzhi-era (1644-1662) memorials were translations from Manchu. The use of feng bing in these Chinese reports may then have pushed the government to adopt feng bing as the official Chinese term for "madness" instead of the more common feng dian 風癲 and feng kuang 風狂, or the more classical diankuang 癲狂 that had been used in Tang and Song law and was still being used in seventeenth-century medicine. A momentous inaccuracy: the 1667 creation of “killing because of madness” The earliest Qing law concerning madness was a terse rule from 1667 mandating that, “Whenever [people with] madness illness kill or injure another, exempt from sentencing” (凡瘋病殺傷人者免議). $ ^{25} $ In its lack of both context and definition, this rule was typical of the “Currently operative regulations of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingbu xianxing zeli 刑部現行則例) of the Kangxi era (1662-1722), but unlike most Kangxi rules, which usually consisted in precedent-based guidelines for modulating the punishment of existing crimes, the 1667 law created a new penal denomination (罪名): “[people with] madness illness killing another” (瘋病殺人), which I shall render as “killing because of madness.” $ ^{26} $
